original_brief.headline: AB1751 Bans Cities from Charging Impact Fees
original_brief.subtext: Infrastructure costs shift entirely to existing taxpayers under new state mandate
original_brief.typographic_element: Bill number AB1751 as oversized display type, upper-left, in terracotta red
original_brief.optional_graphic: Faint outline of California state boundary, centered behind text, warm ivory tones
original_brief.ai_image_prompt: A minimal flat design graphic with a warm ivory background. The central visual element is a faint, clean outline of the state of California rendered in a slightly darker warm cream tone, centered behind where text would appear. A single bold terracotta red horizontal rule or accent bar runs across the lower third of the composition. The overall aesthetic is clean, institutional, and policy-focused — similar to a think tank report cover. No text. No typography. No people. No logos.

briefs.reasoning: The Bill Spotlight needs to feel like a breaking research alert — tight, left-anchored, and data-driven. The bill number AB1751 should dominate as oversized terracotta red display type, functioning as both identifier and visual hook. Unlike the Action Alert (which needs urgency toward a decision) and the Mission Frame (which needs open resolve), this post must feel dense with specific, authoritative information — every typographic zone earns its place. The faint California outline sits behind the text as a ghosted structural element, adding geographic authority without becoming decorative. The composition is compressed and purposeful, not open — this is the most information-dense card of the three.
briefs.square_prompt: Create a 1:1 square format editorial policy graphic for the California Policy Research Center — a nonpartisan research organization. This is a Bill Spotlight card: the visual personality is authoritative, tight, and data-forward, like a policy brief cover breaking an important finding. Background: flat solid warm ivory (#F5F0E2), no gradients, no texture. Behind the text layer, centered in the frame, render a very faint outline-only silhouette of the California state boundary — no fill, a single hairline stroke in a warm mid-tone (approximately #D8CEBF), ghosted to roughly 15% opacity so it reads as a structural whisper, not a decorative feature. It should occupy roughly 60–70% of the frame height, centered vertically and horizontally. The typographic layout is structured in distinct vertical zones, reading top to bottom: ZONE 1 — Upper-left anchor: The bill number 'AB1751' rendered as oversized bold display type in terracotta red (#B84236). This is the dominant visual element and should be dramatically large — approximately 22–26% of the frame height in type size. It sits flush left, beginning approximately 8% from the left edge and 8% from the top edge. It reads first and reads confrontationally — like a file stamp or a breaking data point. ZONE 2 — Directly below AB1751, a thin horizontal rule in terracotta red (#B84236), 2px weight, running from the left margin to approximately 60% of frame width, providing a typographic section break. ZONE 3 — Below the rule, a small category label in terracotta red (#B84236), rendered in small caps or italic at approximately 11–13pt equivalent: 'BILL SPOTLIGHT'. This sits flush left, directly beneath the rule with tight spacing. ZONE 4 — Below the category label, with a clear breathing gap, the headline in deep charcoal (#1C1C1C), bold weight, large but editorially scaled — approximately 13–15% of frame height across its lines, flush left, set in two lines as needed: 'AB1751 Bans Cities from Charging Impact Fees'. This is the second dominant element. ZONE 5 — Below the headline, with generous spacing, the subtext in deep charcoal (#1C1C1C), regular weight, approximately 55–60% of the headline's type size, flush left, set across two lines as needed: 'Infrastructure costs shift entirely to existing taxpayers under new state mandate'. Overall compositional feel: left-anchored, compressed, purposeful. The frame feels filled with structured information — not open or airy. This is the most data-dense card in the weekly set. No photos. No people. No logos. No decorative flourishes. No gradients. Typography only, with the faint California outline as a single structural background element.
briefs.landscape_prompt: Create a 16:9 landscape format editorial policy graphic for the California Policy Research Center — a nonpartisan research organization. This is a Bill Spotlight card: the visual personality is authoritative, tight, and data-forward, like a breaking policy brief header. Background: flat solid warm ivory (#F5F0E2), no gradients, no texture. Behind the text layer, positioned in the right half of the frame — offset slightly right of center, vertically centered — render a very faint outline-only silhouette of the California state boundary. No fill, single hairline stroke in warm mid-tone (approximately #D8CEBF) at roughly 12–15% opacity, occupying approximately 55–65% of frame height. It anchors the right zone without competing with the left-anchored typography. The typographic layout occupies the left 58–62% of the horizontal frame, structured in distinct vertical zones: ZONE 1 — Upper-left: The bill number 'AB1751' rendered as oversized bold display type in terracotta red (#B84236). In the 16:9 format, this can be even more commanding — approximately 20–24% of frame height in type size. Flush left, beginning approximately 6% from the left edge, sitting in the upper third of the frame. It is the immediate visual hook — confrontational, authoritative, large. ZONE 2 — Directly below AB1751, a thin horizontal rule in terracotta red (#B84236), 2px weight, running from the left margin to approximately 45% of total frame width. This is a typographic divider only — not a decorative band. ZONE 3 — Below the rule, a small category label in terracotta red (#B84236), small caps or italic, approximately 11–12pt equivalent: 'BILL SPOTLIGHT'. Flush left, immediately beneath the rule with tight spacing. ZONE 4 — Below the category label, with a clear breathing gap, the headline in deep charcoal (#1C1C1C), bold weight, editorially scaled — approximately 11–13% of frame height across its lines, flush left, constrained within the left typographic column (approximately 55% of frame width), set across two lines as needed: 'AB1751 Bans Cities from Charging Impact Fees'. ZONE 5 — Below the headline, the subtext in deep charcoal (#1C1C1C), regular weight, approximately 55% of headline type size, flush left, within the same left column, two lines as needed: 'Infrastructure costs shift entirely to existing taxpayers under new state mandate'. The right 38–42% of the frame is held primarily by the faint California outline and warm ivory negative space — the horizontal expanse creates a sense of the state as a geographic subject, with the analysis pressing in from the left. Overall feel: split-zone authority. The left zone is dense and structured; the right zone breathes with quiet geographic presence. This creates a distinctly horizontal composition that would feel awkward compressed into a square. No photos. No people. No logos. No decorative flourishes. No gradients.

original_brief.headline: AB2288 Converts Industrial Land to Residential Permanently
original_brief.subtext: Cities cannot require developers to fund infrastructure upgrades for residential use
original_brief.typographic_element: Bill number AB2288 as oversized display type, upper-left, in terracotta red
original_brief.optional_graphic: Abstract split composition — left half suggesting industrial geometry, right half suggesting residential form, both rendered as minimal flat shapes in warm ivory and dark charcoal tones
original_brief.ai_image_prompt: A minimal flat design graphic with a warm ivory background. The composition is divided into two abstract halves: the left side features simple geometric shapes suggesting industrial warehouse or factory forms in dark charcoal, and the right side shows softer residential roofline silhouettes in the same dark charcoal tone. A terracotta red vertical line divides the two halves at the center. The aesthetic is clean, editorial, and policy-focused with no decoration. No text. No typography. No people. No logos.

briefs.reasoning: An Action Alert demands kinetic urgency — the composition must feel like a deadline is pressing in from the left. I'm deploying AB2288 as a massive, oversized terracotta red typographic anchor in the upper-left, creating asymmetric tension by weighting the frame heavily left, with the headline and subtext thrust rightward and downward in a compressed, forward-leaning stack. The abstract split composition — minimal flat industrial geometry (dark charcoal angular shapes) on the left bleeding behind the bill number, dissolving into residential geometric forms (warm ivory pitched-roof silhouettes) on the right — creates horizontal kinetic energy without photos or illustration complexity. The terracotta here is not a thin rule (as in the Bill Spotlight) but a bold, dominant typographic mass — the bill number itself as the accent element, functioning like an alarm signal. This differentiates sharply from Post 1's measured informational register and Post 3's open, resolved negative space.
briefs.square_prompt:
Create a 1:1 square editorial policy graphic for the California Policy Research Center. This is an Action Alert — the composition must feel urgent, compressed, and kinetic, like a deadline is imminent. Background: flat solid warm ivory (#F5F0E2), no gradients, no textures. No photos, no people, no logos, no decorative flourishes.

COMPOSITIONAL STRUCTURE:
The frame is divided into an asymmetric vertical split. On the left third of the frame, running behind and beneath the bill number, place a cluster of minimal flat abstract geometric shapes suggesting industrial architecture — dark charcoal (#1C1C1C) angular forms: a flat rectangle suggesting a factory roofline, a thin vertical chimney stack shape, hard-edged and geometric, minimal. These shapes occupy the left edge and bleed slightly behind the bill number. On the right two-thirds, dissolving toward the right edge, place minimal warm ivory flat geometric shapes with thin charcoal outlines suggesting residential building silhouettes — simple pitched triangular rooflines, rectangular forms — rendered as outlines only, very minimal, ghost-like against the ivory background. These two abstract zones — industrial left, residential right — create a horizontal split tension suggesting land-use transformation. The geometric shapes are background elements only, never competing with typography.

TYPOGRAPHIC ZONES (top to bottom, left-anchored):

ZONE 1 — OVERSIZED BILL NUMBER (dominant anchor, upper-left): The text 'AB2288' rendered in an extremely large, bold, condensed sans-serif typeface. This text is terracotta red (#B84236). It should be dramatically large — roughly 35–40% of the frame height in its cap height — sitting flush to the left margin, upper quadrant of the frame. This is the visual alarm signal. It should feel imposing and immediate.

ZONE 2 — CATEGORY LABEL (just below or beside the bill number, left-aligned): The text 'ACTION ALERT' in small caps, terracotta red (#B84236), small — approximately 12–14pt equivalent. Positioned just below the AB2288 number, left-aligned. Below this label, a thin horizontal terracotta red rule (2px) spanning approximately 60% of the frame width from the left margin, acting as a typographic divider.

ZONE 3 — HEADLINE (below the rule, left-aligned, dominant text block): The exact text 'AB2288 Converts Industrial Land to Residential Permanently' rendered in bold, large display type, deep charcoal (#1C1C1C). This should be set in a tight, compressed stack — roughly 18–22pt equivalent in relative terms, spanning two or three lines, left-aligned. The line breaks should fall naturally. This text sits in the middle third of the frame with clear breathing room above (the rule) and below.

ZONE 4 — SUBTEXT (below the headline, left-aligned, smaller): The exact text 'Cities cannot require developers to fund infrastructure upgrades for residential use' in regular weight, smaller — approximately 55–60% of the headline size — deep charcoal (#1C1C1C). Two lines maximum, left-aligned, with generous space between it and the headline.

OVERALL FEEL: Compressed vertical rhythm, left-heavy asymmetric tension, the oversized red AB2288 as a visual alarm. The industrial-to-residential abstract geometry in the background reinforces the land-use transformation theme without cluttering the typographic hierarchy. Urgency through weight and compression, not chaos. Editorial policy-journal aesthetic under pressure.
briefs.landscape_prompt:
Create a 16:9 landscape editorial policy graphic for the California Policy Research Center. This is an Action Alert — the composition must feel urgent, forward-moving, and kinetic. Background: flat solid warm ivory (#F5F0E2), no gradients, no textures. No photos, no people, no logos, no decorative flourishes.

COMPOSITIONAL STRUCTURE — HORIZONTAL SPLIT LAYOUT:
The landscape format is used to create a strong left-to-right directional tension. The frame is divided into two asymmetric horizontal zones: the left 40% is the typographic engine — heavy, compressed, left-anchored; the right 60% is the abstract visual field — the split industrial-to-residential geometry that breathes and resolves rightward.

LEFT ZONE (left 40% of frame): All typographic elements are anchored here, left-aligned, stacked vertically with tight rhythm. This zone feels dense and weighted.

RIGHT ZONE (right 60% of frame): Abstract background geometric shapes filling this zone. On the far left edge of this right zone (roughly the center of the full frame), place minimal flat dark charcoal (#1C1C1C) angular geometric shapes suggesting industrial architecture — a flat-topped factory silhouette, a thin chimney rectangle, hard geometric edges, flat and minimal. These shapes are darker and more compressed, representing the industrial past. As the shapes move rightward toward the right edge of the frame, they transition — dissolving into minimal residential geometric forms rendered as thin charcoal outlines on warm ivory: simple pitched triangular rooflines, rectangular residential building silhouettes, ghost-like outline-only shapes that grow lighter and more open toward the right edge. This left-to-right transformation — dark industrial to light residential outline — visualizes the land-use conversion described in the bill. These shapes are background layer elements only, never competing with typography.

A thin vertical terracotta red rule (2px, full frame height) marks the boundary between the left typographic zone and the right visual zone, creating a strong structural spine.

TYPOGRAPHIC ZONES within the LEFT ZONE (top to bottom, left-aligned):

ZONE 1 — OVERSIZED BILL NUMBER (dominant anchor, upper-left of the left zone): The text 'AB2288' in an extremely large, bold, condensed sans-serif typeface, terracotta red (#B84236). Cap height should occupy roughly 30–35% of the frame height. Flush left, upper portion of the left zone. This is the visual alarm — oversized, commanding, the first thing the eye lands on.

ZONE 2 — CATEGORY LABEL AND RULE: Immediately below the bill number, left-aligned: the text 'ACTION ALERT' in small caps, terracotta red (#B84236), small — 12–14pt equivalent. Below that, a thin horizontal terracotta red rule (2px) spanning the full width of the left zone.

ZONE 3 — HEADLINE: Below the rule, left-aligned: the exact text 'AB2288 Converts Industrial Land to Residential Permanently' in bold display type, deep charcoal (#1C1C1C). Set in a tight stack — two to three lines, contained within the left zone, not crossing into the right zone. Roughly 16–20pt equivalent in relative scale.

ZONE 4 — SUBTEXT: Below the headline with clear breathing room: the exact text 'Cities cannot require developers to fund infrastructure upgrades for residential use' in regular weight, smaller — approximately 55–60% of headline size — deep charcoal (#1C1C1C). Left-aligned, two lines maximum.

OVERALL FEEL: The left zone is dense, compressed, and urgent — the bill number hits like an alarm. The right zone opens up and breathes, with the industrial-to-residential geometric transformation playing out horizontally across the frame. The vertical terracotta spine separates urgency from implication. The whole composition reads left-to-right with a strong directional pull, creating kinetic forward energy. Editorial policy-journal aesthetic under deadline pressure — not chaotic, but unmistakably time-sensitive.

original_brief.headline: Three Bills Shift Infrastructure Costs to Existing Taxpayers
original_brief.subtext: AB1751, AB2288, and SB1014 each remove developer funding obligations for new growth
original_brief.typographic_element: Three terracotta red vertical bars of equal width, evenly spaced, centered in the lower half of the composition as a structural graphic motif
original_brief.optional_graphic: Three equal vertical columns rendered as bold terracotta red bars, suggesting legislative tallying or a comparative chart structure, minimal and abstract
original_brief.ai_image_prompt: A minimal flat design graphic with a warm ivory background. Three bold terracotta red vertical bars of equal width are evenly spaced across the lower center of the composition, rising to different heights like an abstract bar chart. Above and behind them, a faint dark charcoal horizontal baseline anchors the bars. The overall aesthetic is clean, institutional, and data-driven — resembling a policy think tank infographic without any labeling. No text. No typography. No people. No logos.

briefs.reasoning: The Mission Frame needs to feel expansive and resolved — a synthesis that breathes where the other two posts are compressed and urgent. Where the Bill Spotlight used alarm-scale typography and the Action Alert used tight forward-leaning urgency, this post earns its space through restraint: generous negative space above and below the headline, a philosophical scale that feels declarative rather than reactive. The three terracotta vertical bars function as the graphic centerpiece — a structural, almost architectural motif that visually tallies the three bills while grounding the composition with quiet authority. The bars are deployed in the lower register, allowing the headline to float above them with room to breathe, creating a top-down reading arc from category label → headline → subtext → structural motif. This feels like a journal editorial conclusion, not a data alert.
briefs.square_prompt:
Create a 1:1 square editorial graphic for the California Policy Research Center, a nonpartisan policy research organization. Background is a flat solid warm ivory (#F5F0E2) — no gradients, no textures, no patterns, ever. The overall aesthetic is a policy journal cover: authoritative, measured, spacious, evidence-forward.

COMPOSITIONAL STRUCTURE — read top to bottom in five stacked zones:

ZONE 1 — CATEGORY LABEL (upper portion, vertically centered in the top 15% of the frame): Render the text 'Mission Analysis' in small caps, terracotta red (#B84236), lightweight, approximately 11–13pt equivalent. Center-aligned.

ZONE 2 — THIN HORIZONTAL RULE: Immediately below the category label, a single 2px horizontal rule in terracotta red (#B84236), spanning approximately 55% of the frame width, centered. This is a typographic divider, not a decorative band.

ZONE 3 — HEADLINE (center of frame, spanning roughly 28–32% of frame height across its two lines): Render the exact text 'Three Bills Shift Infrastructure Costs to Existing Taxpayers' in bold display type, deep charcoal (#1C1C1C), center-aligned. The text wraps across two to three lines as needed. This is the dominant typographic element — large, settled, declarative in feeling. Not aggressive or oversized — contained, like a magazine cover headline that announces rather than shouts. Weight is heavy. Generous line spacing.

ZONE 4 — SUBTEXT (below the headline, separated by clear breathing room of approximately 10–12% of frame height): Render the exact text 'AB1751, AB2288, and SB1014 each remove developer funding obligations for new growth' in regular weight, deep charcoal (#1C1C1C), center-aligned, approximately 45–50% of the headline's type size. Two lines of text. This zone should feel subordinate and calm — supplementary evidence, not competing emphasis.

ZONE 5 — GRAPHIC MOTIF (lower 20% of frame, centered): Three vertical bars of equal width and equal height rendered in solid terracotta red (#B84236). Each bar is tall and narrow — approximately 6px wide and 60–70px tall equivalent, or proportionally similar in feel to a tally or comparative chart column. The three bars are evenly spaced with equal gaps between them, centered horizontally as a group. They sit as a quiet structural anchor at the bottom of the composition — abstract, minimal, architectural in feeling. Not decorative. They suggest enumeration and comparative structure.

NEGATIVE SPACE: The ivory background should be abundantly visible — this composition breathes. The top 12% of the frame above the category label is clear ivory. The bottom 8% below the three bars is clear ivory. The space between headline and subtext, and between subtext and the graphic motif, is generous.

STYLE CONSTRAINTS: No photographs. No illustrations. No people. No logos. No gradients. No drop shadows. No decorative flourishes. No photography. Typography and the three vertical bars are the only visual elements. Flat, editorial, structured.
briefs.landscape_prompt:
Create a 16:9 landscape editorial graphic for the California Policy Research Center, a nonpartisan policy research organization. Background is a flat solid warm ivory (#F5F0E2) — no gradients, no textures, no patterns, ever. The overall aesthetic is a policy journal feature opener: authoritative, spacious, resolved, evidence-forward.

COMPOSITIONAL STRUCTURE — horizontal split-zone layout using the wide frame deliberately:

The composition divides into two horizontal regions: a LEFT CONTENT ZONE occupying approximately 62% of frame width (left edge to just past center), and a RIGHT BREATHING ZONE occupying the remaining 38% of frame width. The right zone is clear warm ivory — no text, no elements — functioning as intentional negative space that communicates perspective and resolve. This asymmetry is the defining personality of this post.

WITHIN THE LEFT CONTENT ZONE, read top to bottom in stacked editorial zones:

ZONE 1 — CATEGORY LABEL (upper left, sitting in the top 18% of the frame): Render the text 'Mission Analysis' in small caps, terracotta red (#B84236), lightweight, approximately 11–13pt equivalent. Left-aligned within the left content zone.

ZONE 2 — THIN HORIZONTAL RULE: Immediately below the category label, a single 2px horizontal rule in terracotta red (#B84236), spanning the full width of the left content zone (approximately 62% of total frame width). Left-anchored. This is a typographic divider, not a decorative band.

ZONE 3 — HEADLINE (upper-center of frame, spanning roughly 28–30% of frame height across its lines): Render the exact text 'Three Bills Shift Infrastructure Costs to Existing Taxpayers' in bold display type, deep charcoal (#1C1C1C), left-aligned within the left content zone. The text wraps across two to three lines. This is the dominant typographic element — large, settled, declarative in tone. Weight is heavy but not aggressive. Contained, like a journal feature headline.

ZONE 4 — SUBTEXT (below the headline, separated by generous breathing room): Render the exact text 'AB1751, AB2288, and SB1014 each remove developer funding obligations for new growth' in regular weight, deep charcoal (#1C1C1C), left-aligned within the left content zone, approximately 45–50% of the headline's type size. Two lines. Subordinate, calm, supplementary.

ZONE 5 — GRAPHIC MOTIF (lower left region, bottom 22% of frame, left-aligned within the left content zone): Three vertical bars of equal width and equal height rendered in solid terracotta red (#B84236). Each bar is tall and narrow — proportionally suggesting a tally or minimalist comparative column. The three bars are evenly spaced with equal gaps between them, left-anchored as a group within the left content zone. They sit as a quiet structural anchor at the bottom left of the composition — abstract, architectural, minimal. Not decorative. They suggest enumeration and three-part legislative structure.

RIGHT BREATHING ZONE (rightmost 38% of frame): Completely clear warm ivory (#F5F0E2). No text. No elements. This open space is compositionally intentional — it communicates expansiveness, philosophical scope, and editorial resolve. It differentiates this post from tighter, more data-driven compositions in the weekly set.

NEGATIVE SPACE: Abundant. The ivory background dominates the frame. The top and bottom margins within the left content zone are clear. The right zone is entirely clear. This composition breathes.

STYLE CONSTRAINTS: No photographs. No illustrations. No people. No logos. No gradients. No drop shadows. No decorative flourishes. Typography and the three vertical bars are the only visual elements. Flat, editorial, structured.
